explanation.text: Parrotfish secrete a sticky cocoon that seals their scent from hungry moray eels, and they flee if anything breaks into it. These details show that the cocoon conceals the fish from predators while it rests.
Some diurnal fish adjust their color cells so their bright daytime colors become dull, dark shades at twilight. This change helps them blend into the dim reef and makes them harder to see.
As daylight fades, fish move toward shelters and their bright colors fade or darken. Together, these details show the fishes' natural ways of becoming less visible at night.
A grouper can thrust out its lower jaw until its mouth is large enough to swallow almost any prey. That physical ability explains why a large fish might pose a danger even to a human.
The passage lists sharks' smell, vibration sensing, electric-pulse detection, and low-light vision as ways they find prey. In that context, "zero in on" means to locate a target precisely.

Changes in dusk light cause daytime fish to darken and retreat while predators with dim-light senses become active. This repeated focus shows how reef fish adapt their color, vision, and behavior to the available light.

Predators that waited in the background all day become active hunters at dusk and dawn. This detail belongs in a summary because it captures a major shift in the reef as daytime activity gives way to nighttime life.

The article moves from streams, cellars, snow, and iceboxes to early mechanical refrigerators and modern models. Tracing those methods over many years supports the claim that convenient, dependable cooling took a long time to develop.
Earlier, the author explains that sawdust served as insulation inside an icebox. Packing pond ice in sawdust therefore slowed the transfer of heat and prevented the ice from melting quickly.
The iceman uses tongs to hoist a one-hundred-pound block before carrying it into the house. Because the block must be raised from the cart, "hoist" means lift.
Early refrigerator coolants could leak, and some of the gases were deadly. Manufacturers addressed that safety problem by changing the coolant because a safer gas reduced the danger posed by leaks.

The statement that the middle class could suddenly have things that had seemed high class directly concerns ordinary families. It supports the conclusion that improved refrigeration made previously exclusive foods and comforts available to the average person.

Only about two million households owned refrigerators in 1937, but more than eighty percent had one by the mid-1950s. That sharp rise over time demonstrates that refrigerators gained widespread acceptance.

The article's main history shows that bulky, costly, and dangerous early machines became smaller and safer before ownership spread rapidly. The fact that refrigerators gained popularity after becoming small and affordable therefore captures an essential development, not a minor detail.

The kit is advertised as having everything needed, but the narrator immediately lists nearly every essential item it lacks. This exaggerated contradiction shows why the description is humorous and maintains the story's playful tone.
A starving person would approach a banquet with intense eagerness, just as the narrator attacks the digging job. The comparison therefore emphasizes the depth of his enthusiasm for building the pond.

The instructional video repeatedly presents a water garden as a soothing, peaceful place. By imagining himself "basking in tranquillity," the narrator shows that he believes this vision of relaxed enjoyment will soon become real.

In lines 47 and 48, the narrator observes that few things happen as people think they will. That reflection reveals the story's broader theme because his ideal pond later becomes a muddy trap instead of the peaceful garden he imagined.

Melville is described as landlocked and dry, with only a brief period in its history when water surrounded it. This background explains why even a small new body of water feels especially important to the pond-obsessed narrator.

In lines 74 through 77, the narrator stops seeing a sparkling model pond and recognizes a sticky, muddy trap. This contrast reveals his point of view changing from hopeful imagination to disappointed realism.

When rain first fills the hole, the narrator enthusiastically sees it as the beautiful pond from his plans. By the end, he sees only mud and complains that reality interferes with dreams, showing that excitement has become disappointment.
The passage says that natural fires "have not been allowed to burn" after describing years of suppressing them. That wording indicates that "suppressing" means stopping or preventing the fires.

A prescribed fire burns away heavy undergrowth that could otherwise fuel a destructive wildfire. This evidence supports "fight fire with fire" because a carefully controlled burn reduces the material that would make a later fire more dangerous.
Fire removes old shrub growth and encourages tender new shoots, causing available wildlife food to increase dramatically. These conditions show how a burn can create much more food for animals.
The passage describes forests that have gone too long without fire as especially vulnerable to extremely hot burns. In that context, "prone" means likely to experience such a fire.

Native Americans deliberately used fire to keep grasslands from becoming overgrown with shrubs and trees. Their practice demonstrates to modern land managers how fire helps maintain healthy grassland areas.

The article explains several ways carefully managed fire restores habitats, releases nutrients, and reduces wildfire fuel. Those examples develop the central idea that public-land managers now recognize fire as an important part of nature's cycles.

The final section acknowledges the expense and smoke of prescribed burns, then compares them with the greater costs and pollution of uncontrolled wildfires. It also notes ecological renewal, so the comparison shows that the benefits of planned burns outweigh their costs.
